title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: The Art of the Deal: Haggling Around the World
content.text: Walk through any traditional bazaar in Morocco, a floating market in Thailand, or a street stall in Mexico City, and you will encounter one of humanity's oldest commercial rituals: the art of haggling. Far from being mere penny-pinching, skilled bargaining is a sophisticated social dance that has shaped economies and cultures for thousands of years. (37) [37] Haggling is seen as an essential part of market culture, not just a means to save money. [/37] || In many cultures, setting a fixed price is considered almost rude. Shopkeepers in the Grand Bazaar of Istanbul, for example, would be genuinely offended if a buyer simply paid the first price asked without attempting to negotiate. (38) [38] The back-and-forth exchange is how seller and buyer establish mutual respect and a fair value for goods. [/38] The ritual typically involves an opening offer, theatrical expressions of shock, counter-offers, and eventually a price that both parties find satisfactory. || Western visitors often find this process intimidating or even dishonest, suspecting they will always lose. However, experienced travellers insist that this fear is misplaced. (39) [39] Learning a few phrases in the local language and showing genuine interest in the goods goes a long way toward earning a fair deal. [/39] The secret is to know the approximate local price beforehand, remain cheerful throughout, and be prepared to walk away. || Interestingly, haggling is making something of a comeback even in traditionally fixed-price economies. Car dealerships, electronics retailers and even some high-street boutiques in Europe and North America increasingly expect negotiation. (40) [40] Studies show that simply asking for a discount succeeds surprisingly often in Western retail settings. [/40] || Of course, haggling etiquette varies enormously. In some markets it is perfectly acceptable to offer half the asking price as a starting point; in others that would cause genuine offence. (41) [41] Observing how locals interact with traders before joining in is perhaps the wisest first step for any newcomer. [/41] || Ultimately, haggling is about much more than economics. It is a conversation, a performance and a way of reading another person. (42) [42] Those who master it gain not just lower prices, but a richer, more authentic connection to the places they visit. [/42]

paragraphs.A: In many parts of the world, the transaction itself is considered a social event to be enjoyed.
paragraphs.B: Doing a little research before entering a market is one of the most effective strategies.
paragraphs.C: Respecting local customs and picking up on unspoken rules is therefore crucial.
paragraphs.D: The transaction is not complete until both sides feel the exchange has been worthwhile.
paragraphs.E: It is a skill worth developing, regardless of where in the world you happen to be shopping.
paragraphs.F: This suggests that negotiation skills are universally valuable, even outside traditional bazaars.
paragraphs.G: The price tag, in this view, is merely a starting point for a conversation.

questions.explanation: A reinforces the idea that bargaining is a social event, fitting with the introduction about the commercial ritual.
G explains that the price is only the starting point, connecting the idea that paying without negotiating would be offensive.
B advises researching beforehand, which relates directly to knowing the local price before negotiating.
F concludes that negotiating skills are valuable in any context, summarising the argument about the return of bargaining in the West.
C underlines the importance of respecting local customs, which is exactly what is discussed about differences in bargaining etiquette.
E closes the article by encouraging the development of this skill, supporting the final reflection on the benefits beyond price.
